quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Bond energy (bond dissociation energy)"?

options.text: The energy required to break one mole of a specific bond in the gas phase; always positive (endothermic)
The smallest repeating unit of a crystal lattice; common types include simple cubic, body-centered cubic (BCC), and face-centered cubic (FCC)
The attraction between an ion and a polar molecule; the dominant force when ionic compounds dissolve in water
A measure of molecular polarity; vector sum of all bond dipoles; symmetric molecules (CO₂, CCl₄) can have polar bonds but no net dipole

quiz_questions.explanation: "Bond energy (bond dissociation energy)" is defined as: The energy required to break one mole of a specific bond in the gas phase; always positive (endothermic). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Average bond energy"?

options.text: The average energy to break a particular type of bond across many different molecules; used for ΔH estimation
The distance from the nucleus to the outermost electron shell; decreases across a period (more Zeff), increases down a group (more shells)
The exponent of a concentration term in the rate law; indicates how rate responds to concentration changes (0th, 1st, 2nd)
The temperature and pressure at which all three phases coexist in equilibrium (water: 0.01°C, 611.7 Pa)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Average bond energy" is defined as: The average energy to break a particular type of bond across many different molecules; used for ΔH estimation. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Bond order"?

options.text: ΔTb = iKbm; solute particles raise the boiling point by lowering the vapor pressure; i = van't Hoff factor
Properties that depend on the number of dissolved particles, not their chemical identity: ΔTb, ΔTf, π, ΔP
Strong acids dissociate 100% (Ka very large); weak acids partially dissociate (Ka < 1, typically 10⁻² to 10⁻¹⁰)
The number of shared electron pairs between two atoms: single (1), double (2), triple (3); higher order = shorter, stronger bond

quiz_questions.explanation: "Bond order" is defined as: The number of shared electron pairs between two atoms: single (1), double (2), triple (3); higher order = shorter, stronger bond. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Bond length"?

options.text: The smallest repeating unit of a crystal lattice; common types include simple cubic, body-centered cubic (BCC), and face-centered cubic (FCC)
One compound breaks down into two or more simpler substances: AB → A + B (e.g., 2H₂O₂ → 2H₂O + O₂)
The equilibrium distance between two bonded nuclei; decreases as bond order increases: triple < double < single
If x/[initial] < 5%, the approximation [HA] ≈ [HA]₀ is valid; avoids solving the quadratic equation for weak acid/base equilibria

quiz_questions.explanation: "Bond length" is defined as: The equilibrium distance between two bonded nuclei; decreases as bond order increases: triple < double < single. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "ΔH from bond energies"?

options.text: FC = valence e⁻ - (lone pair e⁻ + ½ bonding e⁻); the best Lewis structure minimizes formal charges
Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons; same Z but different A (e.g., ¹²C, ¹³C, ¹⁴C)
Concentration in moles of solute per liter of solution (mol/L); the most common concentration unit in chemistry
ΔH ≈ Σ(bonds broken) - Σ(bonds formed); positive ΔH if more energy is needed to break bonds than is released forming new ones

quiz_questions.explanation: "ΔH from bond energies" is defined as: ΔH ≈ Σ(bonds broken) - Σ(bonds formed); positive ΔH if more energy is needed to break bonds than is released forming new ones. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The energy required to break one mole of a specific bond in the gas phase; always positive (endothermic)"?

options.text: Bond energy (bond dissociation energy)
Polarizability
Spontaneous process
Heat of vaporization

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Bond energy (bond dissociation energy)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The average energy to break a particular type of bond across many different molecules; used for ΔH estimation"?

options.text: Excess reagent
Average bond energy
Half-reaction method
Crystalline solid

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Average bond energy".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The number of shared electron pairs between two atoms: single (1), double (2), triple (3); higher order = shorter, stronger bond"?

options.text: Mole ratio
Bond order
Selective precipitation
Atomic number (Z)

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Bond order".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The equilibrium distance between two bonded nuclei; decreases as bond order increases: triple < double < single"?

options.text: Lewis structure
Bond length
Secondary battery
Equilibrium vs. completion

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Bond length".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "ΔH ≈ Σ(bonds broken) - Σ(bonds formed); positive ΔH if more energy is needed to break bonds than is released forming new ones"?

options.text: ΔH from bond energies
Temperature change
Pressure/volume change
Limiting reagent

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "ΔH from bond energies".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Bond Energy is accurate?

options.text: The excess reagent remains partially unreacted.
Bond Energy estimates reaction enthalpy from the energies required to break and form chemical bonds. ΔH°rxn ≈ Σ(bond energies broken) - Σ(bond energies formed).
For aA + bB → products, rate = k[A]ᵐ[B]ⁿ, where m and n are reaction orders (determined experimentally, NOT from coefficients).
Q (reaction quotient) compared to K predicts the direction a reaction will shift.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Bond Energy as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Bond Energy?

options.text: Breaking bonds requires energy (endothermic), forming bonds releases energy (exothermic).
Ion-dipole forces are the strongest IMF and govern dissolution of ionic compounds in polar solvents.

Solutions and Concentration describes how solutes dissolve in solvents through solvation. 'Like dissolves like': polar solvents dissolve polar/ionic solutes, nonpolar solvents dissolve nonpolar solutes.
Chemical Bonding connects atoms through ionic, covalent, and metallic bonds.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Bond Energy as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Bond Energy?

options.text: Stoichiometry is the quantitative study of reactants and products in chemical reactions based on the mole concept.
Metallic Bonding and Structures describes how metal atoms share their valence electrons in a 'sea of electrons' that surrounds a lattice of positive metal cations.
Electrochemistry and Redox connects chemical reactions to electrical energy.
Average bond energies are approximations because actual bond energies vary with molecular context.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Bond Energy as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Bond Energy?

options.text: Entropy (S) measures the number of possible microstates (disorder).

Types of Chemical Reactions are classified into several categories: synthesis (A + B → AB), decomposition (AB → A + B), single replacement (A + BC → AC + B), double replacement (AB + CD → AD + CB), and combustion (fuel + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O).
Stronger bonds (triple > double > single) have shorter bond lengths and higher bond energies.
This allows calculation of ΔH for reactions that are difficult to measure directly, by combining known reactions.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Bond Energy as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Bond Energy?

options.text: Ion-dipole forces are the strongest IMF and govern dissolution of ionic compounds in polar solvents.
Bond Energy estimates reaction enthalpy from the energies required to break and form chemical bonds. ΔH°rxn ≈ Σ(bond energies broken) - Σ(bond energies formed).
Dynamic Equilibrium is reached when a reversible reaction's forward and reverse rates become equal, with no net change in concentrations.
Q (reaction quotient) compared to K predicts the direction a reaction will shift.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Bond Energy as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Bond Energy?

options.text: Industrial ammonia production optimizing the ratio of N₂ to H₂ to minimize waste and maximize yield in the Haber process
Road salt (NaCl) lowering the freezing point of water to prevent ice formation on roads—effective down to about -9°C (ΔTf = iKfm, i = 2 for NaCl)
Nitrogen's triple bond (N≡N, 945 kJ/mol) making N₂ extremely unreactive and requiring the high-energy Haber process to fix nitrogen for fertilizers
Table salt (NaCl) as a classic ionic compound: Na donates one electron to Cl, forming Na⁺Cl⁻ with a crystal lattice structure and high melting point (801°C)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Bond Energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Bond Energy?

options.text: Quality control in pharmaceutical manufacturing: acid-base titrations determining the exact concentration of active ingredients in drug formulations
Ozone (O₃) having weaker average O-O bonds (~303 kJ/mol) than O₂ (498 kJ/mol), making it more reactive and useful as a disinfectant and water treatment agent
Swimming pool chemistry: maintaining pH 7.2-7.8 using acid (HCl) or base (NaHCO₃) additions to optimize chlorine disinfection effectiveness
Flame tests and fireworks colors: excited electrons emit characteristic wavelengths when returning to ground state (Na = yellow, Cu = green/blue, Sr = red)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Bond Energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Bond Energy applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Road salt (NaCl) lowering the freezing point of water to prevent ice formation on roads—effective down to about -9°C (ΔTf = iKfm, i = 2 for NaCl)
Fuel energy density comparison using bond energies: hydrogen (H-H = 436 kJ/mol) vs. octane (many C-H and C-C bonds) to predict energy released per gram
Green chemistry principles in industry: catalytic hydrogenation of alkenes (100% atom economy) preferred over reactions producing significant waste byproducts
Water's anomalously high boiling point (100°C vs. H₂S at -60°C) due to extensive hydrogen bonding networks between molecules

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Bond Energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Bond Energy?

options.text: Industrial chemical production (ammonia synthesis: N₂ + 3H₂ → 2NH₃) using stoichiometry to optimize raw material usage and minimize waste
Nitrogen's triple bond (N≡N, 945 kJ/mol) making N₂ extremely unreactive and requiring the high-energy Haber process to fix nitrogen for fertilizers
SN1 and SN2 mechanisms in organic chemistry: SN1 (two-step, carbocation intermediate) vs. SN2 (one-step, bimolecular) determine reaction outcomes in drug synthesis
Stoichiometric calculations in pharmaceutical manufacturing ensuring precise drug composition, maintaining consistent dosage and efficacy

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Bond Energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Bond Energy in practice?

options.text: Electric vehicle batteries: Tesla uses lithium-ion cells (LiNiCoAlO₂ cathode) delivering ~250 Wh/kg, enabling 300+ mile ranges
Electroplating using electrolysis: depositing a thin layer of metal (chrome, gold, nickel) onto objects for corrosion protection or aesthetics
Ozone (O₃) having weaker average O-O bonds (~303 kJ/mol) than O₂ (498 kJ/mol), making it more reactive and useful as a disinfectant and water treatment agent
Blood buffering: CO₂ + H₂O ⇌ H₂CO₃ ⇌ HCO₃⁻ + H⁺ with specific K values maintaining blood pH in the narrow range of 7.35-7.45

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Bond Energy.
